channel.title: NYT > Top Stories
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: In Carroll Lawsuits Inquiry, Scrutiny Turns Toward Private Citizens Who Antagonized Trump
item.description: The Justice Department is said to be examining the funding of lawsuits brought by E. Jean Carroll, an author who has never sought a public role, political power or governmental authority.
item.dc:creator: Glenn Thrush and Benjamin Weiser
item.media:credit: Dave Sanders for The New York Times
item.media:description: E. Jean Carroll, the author and columnist, leaving court in Manhattan in 2024 after a hearing on one of her lawsuits against Donald J. Trump.

item.title: Where Do E. Jean Carroll’s Lawsuits Against President Trump Stand?
item.description: Two monetary judgments against Mr. Trump are winding their way through the legal system, with the Supreme Court likely to be the final word on both.
item.dc:creator: Abbie VanSickle
item.media:credit: Jefferson Siegel for The New York Times
item.media:description: E. Jean Carroll leaves court after closing arguments in her defamation trial against Donald Trump in 2023.

item.title: U.S. Ramps Up Deadly Boat Strikes, and What Trump’s Stock Trades Show
item.description: Plus, the Friday news quiz.
item.dc:creator: Tracy Mumford, Will Jarvis, Margaret Kadifa, Jake Lucas and Ian Stewart
item.category: US Strikes on Alleged Drug Traffickers (2025- )
item.media:credit: Ricardo Arduengo/Reuters
item.media:description: U.S. boat strikes to limit the flow of illicit drugs have included the deployment of F-35 fighter jets, above in Puerto Rico last year, as well as gunships and guided-missile destroyers.

item.title: Trump’s Iran Approach Sows Confusion
item.description: President Trump’s pendulum swings on Iran have often seemed driven by mood and moment rather than any discernible strategy.
item.dc:creator: Michael Crowley and Eric Schmitt
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Doug Mills/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump’s shifts reflect a political tug of war between hawkish supporters urging him to hit Iran harder and noninterventionists urging him to make a quick deal.

item.title: Stranded in the Strait of Hormuz
item.description: We hear from two of the thousands of seafarers who became stuck in the middle of the war.
item.dc:creator: Natalie Kitroeff, Caitlin O’Keefe, Lynsea Garrison, M.J. Davis Lin, Marion Lozano, Dan Powell, Pat McCusker, Elisheba Ittoop and Alyssa Moxley
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Reuters
item.media:description: Thousands of seafarers are stranded in the Persian Gulf, according to the International Maritime Organization, as Iran has effectively blocked the exit through the Strait of Hormuz.

item.title: A Draft U.S.-Iran Plan Is Said to Be on the Table. Here’s What to Know.
item.description: U.S. and Iranian officials say they are closing in on the terms of a preliminary agreement. Yet sticking points, particularly over the Strait of Hormuz, remain.
item.dc:creator: Erika Solomon and Farnaz Fassihi
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi/Polaris for The New York Times
item.media:description: A billboard in Tehran depicting the closure of the Strait of Hormuz in May.

item.title: Trump’s Boat Strikes Have Failed to Curb Cocaine Flow to U.S., Experts Say
item.description: Despite the rising body count off the South American coast, researchers say cocaine is as easy to get in many parts of the United States as it was before the strikes began.
item.dc:creator: Simon Romero
item.category: US Strikes on Alleged Drug Traffickers (2025- )
Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
item.media:description: A screen grab from a video posted by U.S. Southern Command this month showed what the U.S. military said was a vessel on a known narco-trafficking route in the eastern Pacific Ocean.

item.title: After New Push by the Bolsonaros, U.S. Labels Brazilian Gangs as Terrorist Groups
item.description: Following through on threats, the Trump administration has designated Brazil’s two largest drug gangs as terrorist groups.
item.dc:creator: Ana Ionova
item.media:credit: Mauro Pimentel/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Members of the Red Command being arrested last year in Rio de Janeiro. The U.S. designated the gang a terrorist group on Thursday.

item.title: Blue Origin Rocket Blows Up on Florida Launchpad During Test
item.description: The rocket, built by the Jeff Bezos-owned space company, was to carry 48 satellites into space. Blue Origin reported on social media that “all personnel have been accounted for.”
item.dc:creator: Kenneth Chang
item.media:credit: Malcolm Denemark/USA Today, via Imagn Images, via Reuters
item.media:description: The Blue Origin launchpad seen in flames from Cocoa Beach, Fla. on Thursday night.

item.title: The World’s 50 Best Restaurants Announces Its 2026 North America List
item.description: At a ceremony in New Orleans on Thursday, a Midwestern fine-dining standout took the top spot.
item.dc:creator: Julia Moskin

item.title: Russian Drone Hits Romanian Apartment Building, Officials Say
item.description: NATO condemned “Russia’s recklessness” for an episode that sharply escalated tensions between the security alliance and Moscow.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Higgins and Cassandra Vinograd
item.media:credit: Inquam Photos, via Reuters
item.media:description: The site where a drone hit a building in Galati, Romania, on Friday, close to the border with Ukraine.

item.title: The Politics of the Downwardly Mobile Professional Class
item.description: The debate over whether Graham Platner is “working class” comes at a time when more and more people are at risk of falling into it.
item.dc:creator: Noam Scheiber

item.title: Times/Siena Poll: 43% of Voters Dissatisfied With Both Parties
item.description: Persistent frustration over the economy and foreign policy have left many Americans feeling politically homeless, and young voters are particularly frustrated.
item.dc:creator: Ruth Igielnik
item.media:credit: Michael Swensen for The New York Times
item.media:description: The poll found that just 26 percent of voters felt satisfied with the Democratic Party and that 33 percent felt satisfied with the Republican Party.

item.title: California House Primary in Sacramento Displays Democrats’ Fierce Generational Battle
item.description: Representative Doris Matsui has been re-elected 10 times with ease. But an upstart Democrat is threatening to end her run in Congress.
item.dc:creator: Laurel Rosenhall
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: Representative Doris Matsui is facing her toughest re-election fight in her more than two decades of representing a safely Democratic district in the Sacramento region.

item.title: Why Hasn’t California Elected a Woman Governor?
item.description: Most states have had a woman serve as governor, but California is in the minority despite its progressive-minded electorate. Women who have reached the top elsewhere saw familiar roadblocks.
item.dc:creator: Heather Knight, Soumya Karlamangla and Jill Cowan
item.media:credit: Gabriela Bhaskar/The New York Times
item.media:description: Katie Porter, a Democrat who served three terms in Congress, is the final prominent woman in the California governor’s race.

item.title: Natural Light. Soaring Ceilings. At Penn Station? That’s the Plan.
item.description: A week after Amtrak selected a developer to remake the dreary rail hub, details of its rebirth are emerging. The cost is less clear.
item.dc:creator: Patrick McGeehan
item.category: Moynihan Train Hall at Pennsylvania Station (Manhattan, NY)
item.media:credit: Todd Heisler/The New York Times
item.media:description: An overhaul of Pennsylvania Station in New York could cost $7 billion.

item.title: Scripps Spelling Bee 2026 Takeaways: Shrey Parikh Wins in Spell-Off
item.description: The competition came down to a spell-off. Contestants were eliminated early, and a sixth grader became a crowd favorite.
item.dc:creator: Maria Cramer
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times

item.title: A.I. Doesn’t Have to Mean Layoffs
item.description: A French multinational, Schneider Electric, decided to use artificial intelligence in manufacturing to make workers more productive, rather than to replace them. Here’s how that’s going.
item.dc:creator: Patricia Cohen and Alexis Pazoumian

item.title: Europe Is Edging Closer to a Trade War With China. Here’s Why.
item.description: As cheap goods pour in, threatening the continent’s manufacturing sector, a search for solutions is becoming increasingly urgent.
item.dc:creator: Jeanna Smialek and Alexandra Stevenson
item.media:credit: Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Chinese-made electric vehicles awaiting export at the port in Hangzhou, eastern China, in May.

item.title: ‘Hacks’ Leaves Them Laughing
item.description: In an interview, Jean Smart and Hannah Einbinder discuss the series finale, their offscreen relationship and why comedy is just like sex.
item.dc:creator: Alexis Soloski and Chantal Anderson
item.media:credit: Chantal Anderson for The New York Times

item.title: ‘Hacks’ Was Always a Love Story
item.description: In its finale, this comedy about comedy circled back to the romance of creative partnership and the saving power of laughs.
item.dc:creator: James Poniewozik

item.title: The Knicks’ Playoff Run Has Brought a Peculiar Harmony to New York
item.description: The team’s playoff run has brought a curious sense of amity to the city.
item.dc:creator: Matt Flegenheimer
item.media:credit: José A. Alvarado Jr. for The New York Times
item.media:description: A blazing playoff run has electrified New York Knicks fans of all ages, in a city unaccustomed to such things from its basketball team.

item.title: Hungary Showed How to Defeat an Autocrat
item.description: Hungary did it. The United States can, too.
item.dc:creator: M. Gessen and Máté Bartha
item.media:credit: Máté Bartha for The New York Times

item.title: In a World That Enabled Epstein, What Makes a Good Man?
item.description: A much-needed, nuanced conversation about masculinity and feminism today.
item.dc:creator: Nadja Spiegelman, Frederick Joseph, Ruth Whippman and Vishakha Darbha
item.media:credit: Illustration by The New York Times; photo by Raul Arboleta/Getty

item.title: Jimmy Kimmel Finds Glee in President Trump’s Approval Rating
item.description: A new poll shows it sitting at 34 percent. “He has the same approval rating as ‘Paul Blart: Mall Cop,’” Kimmel said on Thursday.
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix

item.title: France’s Far Right Has 2 Leaders. They Don’t Always Have One Voice.
item.description: Marine Le Pen and her protégé, Jordan Bardella, lead presidential polls. They are united on curbing immigration but split recently on economic policy.
item.dc:creator: Mark Landler
item.media:credit: Sameer Al-Doumy/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Jordan Bardella and Marine Le Pen at a National Rally event in March. Spotting daylight between them has become a preoccupation for French journalists and analysts.

item.title: Trans Athlete Could Repeat as State Champion at California Meet
item.description: Special rules will again be in place. Podium spots might be shared.
item.dc:creator: Juliet Macur
item.media:credit: Adam Perez for The New York Times
item.media:description: AB Hernandez, a senior at Jurupa Valley High School, is considered a strong contender to win three events at the state championships on Friday and Saturday in Clovis, Calif.

item.title: Suit Says Black Infants Were Subjected to Experimental Vaccine Without Consent
item.description: The babies were part of a vaccine trial for a respiratory virus in the 1960s and died shortly after. Their families said they had been unaware of the trial until recently.
item.dc:creator: Remy Tumin
item.media:credit: Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Benjamin Crump is representing the families of two Black infants who were unknowingly enrolled as test subjects in a mid-1960s vaccine trial for R.S.V.
